Title: Characterization of the galactono-1,4-lactone dehydrogenase from pepper fruits and its modulation in the ascorbate biosynthesis. Role of nitric oxide

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antioxidant important for plant physiology and human nutrition; vitamin C source in diet
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Fruits (green and red pepper fruits showed the highest ascorbate levels among organs tested)
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Terminal step of the L-galactose pathway
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: FAD-binding-4; GlcD-like FAD/FMN dehydrogenase domain; MurB-related binding domain
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Mitochondrial inner membrane; associated with mitochondria via an N-terminal transmembrane helix
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highest transcript levels in leaves and flowers; lower in fruits and stems; expression maintained during fruit development/ripening; strongly induced by NO treatment in fruits
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highest transcript levels in leaves and flowers; lower in fruits and stems; induced about 13-fold in breaking-point fruits after NO treatment and measured in red ripe fruits
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes the final step of ascorbate biosynthesis by oxidizing L-galactono-1,4-lactone to ascorbic acid, transferring electrons to cytochrome c
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a key control/modulation point in pepper ascorbate biosynthesis, but not the only limitation step in the pathway
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes ascorbate accumulation in pepper fruits; NO-induced upregulation/activity increase correlates with about 40–42% higher fruit ascorbate
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Full-length pepper GalLDH cDNA was cloned and identified as the terminal enzyme of the L-galactose ascorbate pathway. 2. GalLDH protein is a mitochondrial, membrane-associated flavoprotein with a 37-aa targeting peptide and one main N-terminal transmembrane helix. 3. GalLDH activity pattern across organs paralleled ascorbate content better than transcript levels, supporting a key role in pepper physiology and ascorbate homeostasis. 4. Exogenous NO increased GalLDH transcript (~13-fold), enzyme activity (~3-fold), and fruit ascorbate (~40–42%). 5. In vitro SIN-1 and GSNO did not alter activity, indicating NO regulation is transcriptional/post-transcriptional rather than via nitration or S-nitrosylation of GalLDH.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Full-length cDNA cloning and sequencing; qRT-PCR; enzyme activity assay with L-galactono-1,4-lactone/cytochrome c; immunoblotting with anti-GalLDH antibody; antibody inhibition assay; ascorbate quantification; NO treatment of fruits; in vitro donor assays with SIN-1, GSNO and GSH
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fruit ripening stage; exogenous nitric oxide atmosphere (5 ppm, 1 h, then 10 d); in vitro treatment with SIN-1, GSNO, and GSH
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Cytochrome c; mitochondrial respiratory Complex I (NADH dehydrogenase complex)
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: GalLDH produced a ~56 kDa immunoreactive band in green and red fruits. Activity was reduced by anti-GalLDH antibody and significantly inhibited by GSH (~60%), but unaffected by SIN-1 or GSNO. The paper notes upstream enzymes GMP/VTC1 and GGP/VTC2 are also important regulatory points, but they were not core experimental subjects here.
